title: Adirondack Chair
summary: Curved back, sloped seat, cedar throughout. The angles are the whole design — cut the template right and the rest is assembly.
description:
Everyone thinks the Adirondack chair is about the curved back. It isn't. It's about two angles: the seat slopes back about 15°, and the back leans about 20° off that. Get those wrong and you've built a chair that's either impossible to get out of or actively hostile to sit in.

So: make a hardboard template for the curved seat support, and make it once. Every chair after the first one is a two-hour build.

Cedar for weather resistance. Stainless screws, because plain steel will bleed rust streaks down a cedar chair in one wet season.
timeLabel: 7–10 hrs for the first one, 3 for the second

tools.note: All the curves. A band saw is better if you have one.
You will sand a lot of curved edges. Budget time for it.
A flush-trim bit plus your template makes identical parts. Worth it if you build more than one.

materials.name: Cedar, 2x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Legs and seat supports — the structural parts.

materials.name: Stainless steel screws, #8 x 1-1/4" and 2"
materials.note: Stainless, not coated. Cedar's tannins eat plain steel and streak rust down the chair.

materials.name: Exterior finish or leave raw
materials.note: Optional. Raw cedar silvers to grey and lasts fine. Finish means re-finishing, forever.

cutList.note: The curved part. Cut from the template. This piece defines the chair's whole geometry.
Spaced ~1/2" apart so rain drains through.
Cut to a fan curve at the top — tallest in the center.
Wide enough to hold a drink. This is the single most appreciated feature of the chair.

steps.title: Make the template first
steps.body:
Draw the curved rear leg/seat support full size on hardboard. The seat section slopes back ~15°; the back support rises at ~20° off the seat.

Cut it, sand the curve fair, and **sit on a mockup before you cut cedar.** Clamp two scraps together at your angles and try it. Adjust until it's genuinely comfortable. Everything else in this build is downstream of this template.

steps.title: Cut the two rear legs
steps.body:
Trace the template onto your 2x4 stock, twice. Cut with a jigsaw, staying just outside the line, then sand to the line — or rout flush with the template and a flush-trim bit.

The two must be identical. If they're not, the chair rocks.

steps.title: Build the seat frame
steps.body:
Attach the front legs to the rear leg assemblies. Add a front cross-brace.

Check it sits flat on the floor with no wobble before you go further. Fix a wobble now, not after 13 slats are on.

steps.title: Attach seat slats
steps.body:
Screw the six seat slats across the curved seat section, front to back, with ~1/2" gaps.

The gaps aren't a style choice — water has to get out, or the chair rots from the seat down.

steps.title: Fan the back slats
steps.body:
Cut the back slats to a fan: tallest in the middle, stepping down toward the outside, with the tops rounded over.

Screw them to the two back braces — one low, one high — with even gaps. Do the layout on the ground first, get the fan symmetric, then attach.

steps.title: Arms last
steps.body:
Screw the arm supports to the front legs, then land the arms on top of them and into the back. Round over the front corners of the arms generously — they're what your forearm rests on for hours.

Make sure the arms are level with each other and with the ground.

steps.title: Sand everything, then sit in it
steps.body:
Sand every edge and corner you can reach — this is a chair people sit in wearing shorts. Nothing sharp survives.

Leave the cedar raw and let it silver, or finish it and accept that you've signed up to redo that every couple of years. Raw is the honest answer.

Build the second one. The template's already made and it goes three times faster.
